name: Tepache
subtitle: Mexico’s pre-Columbian pineapple fizzer
blrNote: Pineapples are ₹60–80 year-round at any local pushcart. Use Mandya jaggery (Bella) instead of Mexican piloncillo-it’s functionally identical and adds a gorgeous mineral depth.

ingredients.name: Pineapple skin & core
substitutions: Organic is better to ensure wild yeast

IN.where: Any fruit stall - keep the scraps after eating the fruit

tldr: Pineapple scraps + jaggery + water. Wait 2 days. Drink the sun.

steps.title: Prep the scraps
steps.instruction: Wash the pineapple exterior lightly (don’t scrub off the yeast). Remove the skin in large strips and keep the core. Eat the flesh.
tips: Do not scrub the skin with soap - the white dusty layer on the outside contains the wild yeasts that drive fermentation
Organic or unsprayed pineapple is ideal; if using conventional, a light rinse is enough
Organic pineapple carries a richer population of wild yeast on its skin, which leads to a more vigorous and flavourful ferment

steps.title: Dissolve & Mix
steps.instruction: Dissolve jaggery in 2L water. Add the skins, core, and cinnamon stick to the jar.
tips: Dissolve jaggery in warm water first, then let it cool before adding pineapple - hot water kills the wild yeasts on the skin
Mandya bella (jaggery) works identically to Mexican piloncillo and adds a rich mineral sweetness
Make sure the water has cooled to below 35°C before combining - anything hotter will kill the wild yeasts on the pineapple skin and stall fermentation

steps.title: Ferment
steps.instruction: Cover with cloth. At BLR’s 25°C, it will bubble aggressively in 24h. Skim the white foam daily.
steps.checkpoint: Smells like fermented honey and pineapple
tips: The white foam on top is normal yeast activity - skim it but do not worry about it
Taste daily: sweet-tart with fizz means ready; if it tastes like vinegar, you have gone too far (but save it - pineapple vinegar is great)
In BLR summer (30°C+), check at 18 hours - it can go from perfect to vinegar fast

steps.title: Bottle & Chill
steps.instruction: Strain and bottle. Refrigerate immediately. If left out, it becomes pineapple vinegar (also useful!).
tips: Use flip-top bottles and burp them twice daily for the first 2 days in the fridge - carbonation continues building
Serve over ice with a pinch of chilli powder and a squeeze of lime for the authentic Mexican street style
Leave at least 3cm of headroom in each bottle - carbonation continues building in the fridge and overfilled bottles can burst

culturalContext.story: Tepache dates back to Pre-Columbian Mexico, where the Nahua people fermented corn-based beverages called 'tepatli.' After the Spanish introduced pineapple cultivation, the drink evolved into the pineapple-rind version we know today. In Mexico City, dedicated 'Tepacherías' serve it ice-cold in plastic bags with a straw, often spiked with a splash of beer. Street vendors across Mexico sell it from large glass barrels (vitroleros) for just a few pesos - it is the people's drink, cheap, refreshing, and alive.
culturalContext.historicalNote: The word 'tepache' derives from the Nahuatl 'tepiatl' meaning 'drink made from corn.' The shift to pineapple happened in the colonial era when tropical fruits became widely available. The original corn-based tepache still exists in some indigenous communities in Oaxaca and Guerrero.
culturalContext.significance: It is the ultimate zero-waste ferment. You pay for the fruit, but the drink is made from what usually goes in the bin. In an era of food waste awareness, tepache is a centuries-old solution to a modern problem.
culturalContext.funFact: In some parts of Mexico, a pinch of salt and chilli powder is added to the glass before serving - the same chamoy-tajin treatment given to fresh fruit.

thingsToAccountFor.title: The Explosive Factor
thingsToAccountFor.description: At 28°C+ in Bengaluru, carbonation builds FAST. If you bottle this, "burp" the bottles twice a day or they will explode.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Vinegar tipping point
thingsToAccountFor.description: Tepache transitions from sweet-fizzy to vinegar quickly, especially in warm climates. Taste every 12 hours after the first 24. Once it is pleasantly tart and fizzy, strain and refrigerate immediately.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Avoid metal containers
thingsToAccountFor.description: The acidic environment of tepache reacts with metal, producing off-flavours and potentially leaching harmful compounds. Use glass, food-grade plastic, or ceramic only.

dehydratorIntegration.result: Fermented pineapple "leather" bits-great for infusing into spirits.

variations.name: Tepache con Chile
variations.description: Add one slit green chilli during fermentation for a spicy kick.

variations.name: Tepache con Tamarindo
variations.description: Add 50g tamarind pulp along with the pineapple scraps. Produces a darker, tangier drink with a deeper sour note. Popular in Oaxaca and southern Mexico.

variations.name: Ginger Tepache (BLR Fusion)
variations.description: Replace cinnamon with a 5cm knob of crushed fresh ginger. The ginger adds a sharp warmth that works beautifully with Bangalore's climate and pairs well with Indian food.
